bogles_folly.title: Bogle's Folly - The First Index Fund
bogles_folly.story: When I launched the first index fund in 1976, Wall Street called it 'Bogle's Folly.' They said it was un-American. That a fund designed just to match the market was 'a sure path to mediocrity.' The IPO was supposed to raise $150 million - we barely got $11 million. But I knew the math. Most active managers lose to the market after fees. We stayed the course, and eventually the world caught up. Sometimes the best ideas look like the worst ones at first.
bogles_folly.lesson: The best ideas often face the most resistance. Trust the math, stay the course.

princeton_thesis.title: The Princeton Thesis
princeton_thesis.story: In 1951, I was a senior at Princeton writing my thesis. I studied the mutual fund industry and found something surprising - most fund managers didn't beat the market after fees. My professors were skeptical. The industry was furious. But the data was clear. Twenty-five years later, I built Vanguard on that insight. Sometimes the most important ideas come to you early. The key is having the patience to see them through.
princeton_thesis.lesson: Evidence matters more than opinion. And young people sometimes see what experts miss.

heart_transplant.title: The Second Chance
heart_transplant.story: In 1996, I received a heart transplant. I was 67 years old, and the doctors had told me decades earlier that I wouldn't live past 40. Every day since has been a gift. I think about my donor often - someone who gave me the chance to keep working, keep teaching, keep being with my family. That experience taught me what really matters. It's not the money. It's the time we have and how we use it.
heart_transplant.lesson: Time is our most precious asset. Don't waste it worrying about things that don't matter.

the_firing.title: Getting Fired to Start Vanguard
the_firing.story: In 1974, I was fired from Wellington Management - the company I'd dedicated my career to. I'd made a bad merger decision, and I paid for it. I was devastated. But that failure forced me to create something new. I founded Vanguard, structured it so that shareholders would own the company, not outside executives. The worst moment of my career led to my greatest contribution. Sometimes failure is just redirection.
the_firing.lesson: Setbacks can become springboards. The door that closes may open a better one.

meeting_investors.title: The Letters
meeting_investors.story: Throughout my career, I answered my own mail. Thousands of letters from ordinary investors - teachers, nurses, small business owners - telling me that Vanguard helped them retire, send kids to college, feel secure. Those letters meant more to me than any award or recognition. Building wealth isn't about numbers on a screen. It's about real people living better lives. That's what keeps me going.
meeting_investors.lesson: The real measure of success is the difference you make in others' lives.
